name: Shakey the Robot
short_description: The first mobile robot to integrate perception, planning, and action — an AI-driven wheeled platform that could decompose high-level goals into physical sub-tasks.
long_description: Developed at SRI International's Artificial Intelligence Center from 1966 to 1972, Shakey was the first robot to reason about its environment and plan sequences of physical actions autonomously. Equipped with a television camera, sonar range finders, bump detectors, and a radio link to off-board computers running LISP, Shakey used the STRIPS planner to decompose instructions like 'push the block off the platform' into goal-directed sub-tasks — navigating rooms, toggling light switches, and pushing objects. Its development produced two foundational algorithms: the A* search method for pathfinding and the STRIPS planning formalism that became a cornerstone of AI planning research. While primarily a navigation and reasoning testbed, Shakey's use of pushing to manipulate objects made it the earliest working example of planning-integrated physical manipulation. Inducted into the Robot Hall of Fame in 2004 and awarded an IEEE Milestone in 2017, it now resides at the Computer History Museum.
ai_note: Shakey used the STRIPS logical planner and A* search to reason about goals and generate plans — genuine symbolic AI at the core of its operation, not scripted playback.

images.caption: Shakey the Robot (developed 1966–1972 at SRI International), photographed at the Computer History Museum, 1969.
images.alt_text: A tall wheeled robot with a boxy white chassis, a cylindrical sensor mast topped with a camera, and a radio antenna, displayed in a museum setting against a neutral background.

sources.title: Shakey the Robot
sources.access_notes: Official SRI heritage page. Describes Shakey as the first mobile robot with the ability to perceive and reason about its surroundings; notes active period 1966–1972 and current residence at the Computer History Museum.

sources.title: Shakey the robot
sources.access_notes: Comprehensive overview: team (Rosen, Nilsson, Hart, Fikes, Raphael, Duda, and others), sensors (TV camera, sonar, bump detectors, radio), software (STRIPS planner, LISP, A* search, Hough transform), capabilities, DARPA funding, and IEEE Milestone (2017).

sources.title: Shakey: First Mobile Reasoning Robot
sources.access_notes: CHM Revolution exhibition entry. Confirms Shakey could understand natural language instructions such as 'push the block off the platform,' plan autonomously, and execute sub-tasks. Notes A* and STRIPS as key algorithmic outputs.

interactions.title: Type a goal, watch it figure out the steps
interactions.description: A researcher sat at a console and typed a terse instruction such as "push the block off the platform." Rather than being walked through each step, Shakey broke the goal into its own sub-tasks and carried them out, the first robot to bridge typed instruction and physical action.

interactions.title: Improvise a ramp to reach a block it can't touch
interactions.description: Told to push a block off a raised platform it couldn't reach from the floor, Shakey located a ramp, pushed it against the platform, rolled up it, found the block, and shoved it off — a multi-step plan it assembled itself.

interactions.title: Compound errands across rooms
interactions.description: Operators gave it layered goals like "go to room D and push block 9 over to where doorway 4 is," combining navigation, object identification, and pushing into a single instruction the planner had to untangle.

interactions.title: Instruct it in a slice of plain English
interactions.description: Shakey understood roughly 100 words of written English; a typed instruction was translated into a verbal code and then into the formal expressions its planner reasoned over, all running on a PDP-10 in another room linked by radio.

interactions.title: Send it to flip a light switch or open a door
interactions.description: Its action repertoire included moving between locations, toggling light switches on and off, opening and closing doors, climbing over rigid objects, and pushing movable ones — the building blocks the planner strung together.

interactions.title: Find its own way through a cluttered room
interactions.description: Steering by the baseboards and pausing at open doorways, Shakey could pick a path through an arrangement of obstacles and later recall it to return by a different route — early autonomous route-finding rather than a fixed track.

interactions.title: Stage a tidy-the-room demo
interactions.description: Given its block-pushing and room-to-room navigation, an operator could type a goal like consolidating scattered blocks against one wall and let the planner decide the order of pushes and routes — a clean-up errand built entirely from actions Shakey already had.

interactions.title: Inspection round at lights-out
interactions.description: Drawing on its move-and-toggle repertoire, Shakey could be told to visit each room in turn and switch the lights off, planning a route that hits every switch — a nightly walk-through expressed as one high-level goal.
